change if we integrate over any interval of length $2 \pi$ (see Theorem 1, tion 7.1). In the next example, we use this technique of even functions well as a double-angle identity and the linearity property of the integ These will simplify the residue calculation.

EXAMPLE 3 Techniques of integration
Compute the definite integral (Figure 6)
$$
I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos 2 \theta}{1+2 \cos ^{2} \theta} d \theta
$$

Solution First, use the double angle identity $2 \cos ^{2} \theta=1+\cos 2 \theta$, then the cha of variables $\theta^{\prime}=2 \theta$, and get (for convenience, rename $\theta^{\prime}$ by $\theta$ )
$$
I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos 2 \theta}{2+\cos 2 \theta} d \theta=\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{\cos \theta}{2+\cos \theta} d \theta
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
I & =\frac{1}{4} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{2+\cos \theta-2}{2+\cos \theta} d \theta \\
& =\frac{1}{4} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} d \theta+\frac{1}{4} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{-2}{2+\cos \theta} d \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{d \theta}{2+\cos \theta}
\end{aligned}
$$

(0)
ite integral
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\text { This integral is over }[0, \pi] \text {, but the integrand is even, so according to (6) we ha } \\
\qquad I=\frac{1}{4} \int_{0}^{2 \pi} \frac{\cos \theta}{2+\cos \theta} d \theta \\
\text { This integral could be evaluated by the use of residues, but we can significa } \\
\text { reduce the amount of residue calculation by using linearity: }
\end{array}
$$

We now evaluate this last integral by the residue method. Letting $z=e^{i \theta}$, co $\frac{1}{2}\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right), d \theta=\frac{d z}{i z}$, we obtain
$$
I=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{1}{i} \int_{C_{1}(0)} \frac{d z}{z^{2}+4 z+1}
$$
th and poles tegral in Ex-

The integrand $\frac{1}{z^{2}+4 z+1}=\frac{1}{\left(z-z_{1}\right)\left(z-z_{2}\right)}$ has simple poles at $z_{1}=-2-\sqrt{3} z_{2}=-2+\sqrt{3}$, and $z_{2}$ is the only pole lying inside $C_{1}(0)$ (Figure 7). The re there is
$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{1}{\left(z-z_{1}\right)\left(z-z_{2}\right)}, z_{2}\right) & =\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{2}}\left(z-z_{2}\right) \frac{1}{\left(z-z_{1}\right)\left(z-z_{2}\right)} \\
& =\frac{1}{z_{2}-z_{1}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}
\end{aligned}
$$
and so $I=\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{1}{i} 2 \pi i \frac{1}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\pi\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$.
Although the integrals in this section are special, they have impo applications, including the computation of certain Fourier series. See tion 7.2 for illustrations.
